content:
# Luke Chapter 14 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 106-107 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_014_page_001.jpg, LUK_014_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And it came to pass, when he entered into the house of one of the rulers of the Pharisees on a Sabbath to eat bread, that they were watching him closely.

**2** And behold, a certain man who had dropsy was before him.

**3** And Jesus answered and spoke to the lawyers and Pharisees, saying, "Is it lawful to heal on the Sabbath, or not?"

**4** But they were silent. And he took hold of him and healed him and let him go.

**5** And he said to them, "Which of you, if a son or an ox shall fall into a well, will not immediately pull him out on the day of the Sabbath?"

**6** And they were not able to answer him concerning these things.

**7** And he spoke a parable to those who had been invited, observing how they were choosing the first seats, saying to them,

**8** "When you are invited by someone to a wedding feast, do not recline in the first seat, lest someone more honorable than you may have been invited by him,

**9** and the one who invited you and him shall come and say to you, 'Give this one the place,' and then you begin with shame to take the last place.

**10** But when you are invited, go and recline in the last place, so that when the one who has invited you comes, he may say to you, 'Friend, go up higher.' Then you shall have glory before all who recline with you.

**11** For everyone who exalts himself shall be humbled, and he who humbles himself shall be exalted."

**12** And he said also to the one who had invited him, "When you make a dinner or a supper, do not call your friends, nor your brothers, nor your kinsmen, nor your rich neighbors, lest they also invite you in return and a repayment be made to you.

**13** But when you make a feast, invite the poor, the maimed, the lame, the blind;

**14** and you shall be blessed, because they do not have the means to repay you; for it shall be repaid to you in the resurrection of the righteous."

**15** And when one of those who reclined at table with him heard these things, he said to him, "Blessed is he who shall eat bread in the kingdom of God."

**16** But he said to him, "A certain man was making a great supper, and he invited many;

**17** and he sent his servant at the hour of the supper to say to those who had been invited, 'Come, for all things are now ready.'

**18** And they all with one accord began to make excuse. The first said to him, 'I have bought a field, and I have need to go out and see it; I ask you, have me excused.'

**19** And another said, 'I have bought five yoke of oxen, and I am going to prove them; I ask you, have me excused.'

**20** And another said, 'I have married a wife, and on account of this I am not able to come.'

**21** And the servant came and reported these things to his lord. Then the master of the house, being angry, said to his servant, 'Go out quickly into the streets and lanes of the city, and bring in here the poor and maimed and blind and lame.'

**22** And the servant said, 'Lord, what you commanded has been done, and still there is room.'

**23** And the lord said to the servant, 'Go out into the roads and hedges and compel them to come in, so that my house may be filled.

**24** For I say to you, that none of those men who were invited shall taste of my supper.'"

**25** And great crowds were going with him, and he turned and said to them,

**26** "If anyone comes to me and does not hate his own father and mother and wife and children and brothers and sisters, and yet also his own life, he is not able to be my disciple.

**27** Whoever does not bear his own cross and come after me is not able to be my disciple.

**28** For which of you, wishing to build a tower, does not first sit down and count the cost, whether he has enough to complete it?

**29** Lest, having laid a foundation and not being able to finish, all who see it begin to mock him,

**30** saying, 'This man began to build and was not able to finish.'

**31** Or what king, going to encounter another king in war, will not sit down first and take counsel whether he is able with ten thousand to meet the one who comes against him with twenty thousand?

**32** And if not, while the other is yet far off, he sends an embassy and asks the terms of peace.

**33** So therefore, every one of you who does not renounce all his own possessions is not able to be my disciple.

**34** Salt therefore is good; but if even the salt becomes tasteless, with what shall it be seasoned?

**35** It is fit neither for the earth nor for the dunghill; they cast it out. He who has ears to hear, let him hear."

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Manuscript Verification

The two Sinaiticus manuscript images (pages 106-107) were examined. Page 1 covers approximately Luke 14:1 through Luke 14:26 across four columns. Page 2 covers approximately Luke 14:27 through Luke 15:22 across four columns, with Luke 14 concluding in the first column. The text is in Sinaiticus's characteristic continuous uncial script, four columns per page. Key readings confirmed from the manuscript:

- ΕΙΣ ΟΙΚΟΝ ΤΙΝΟΣ ΤΩΝ ΑΡΧΟΝΤΩΝ ΤΩΝ ΦΑΡΙΣΑΙΩΝ (14:1, "into the house of one of the rulers of the Pharisees")
- ΗΣΑΝ ΠΑΡΑΤΗΡΟΥΜΕΝΟΙ ΑΥΤΟΝ (14:1, "they were watching him closely" — periphrastic imperfect)
- ΥΔΡΩΠΙΚΟΣ (14:2, "dropsical/having dropsy" — NT hapax legomenon)
- ΤΟΥΣ ΝΟΜΙΚΟΥΣ ΚΑΙ ΦΑΡΙΣΑΙΟΥΣ (14:3, "the lawyers and Pharisees")
- ΥΙΟΣ Η ΒΟΥΣ (14:5, "a son or an ox" — significant variant; other MSS read ΟΝΟΣ, "donkey")
- ΤΑΣ ΠΡΩΤΟΚΛΙΣΙΑΣ ΕΞΕΛΕΓΟΝΤΟ (14:7, "they were choosing the first seats")
- ΠΑΣ Ο ΥΨΩΝ ΕΑΥΤΟΝ ΤΑΠΕΙΝΩΘΗΣΕΤΑΙ (14:11, "everyone who exalts himself shall be humbled")
- ΠΤΩΧΟΥΣ ΑΝΑΠΕΙΡΟΥΣ ΧΩΛΟΥΣ ΤΥΦΛΟΥΣ (14:13, "poor, maimed, lame, blind")
- ΕΝ ΤΗ ΑΝΑΣΤΑΣΕΙ ΤΩΝ ΔΙΚΑΙΩΝ (14:14, "in the resurrection of the righteous")
- ΔΕΙΠΝΟΝ ΜΕΓΑ (14:16, "a great supper")
- ΑΠΟ ΜΙΑΣ (14:18, "with one accord" — elliptical expression)
- ΓΥΝΑΙΚΑ ΕΓΗΜΑ (14:20, "I have married a wife" — no polite formula)
- ΟΡΓΙΣΘΕΙΣ Ο ΟΙΚΟΔΕΣΠΟΤΗΣ (14:21, "the master of the house, being angry")
- ΤΑΣ ΟΔΟΥΣ ΚΑΙ ΦΡΑΓΜΟΥΣ (14:23, "the roads and hedges")
- ΑΝΑΓΚΑΣΟΝ (14:23, "compel" — urgent persuasion, not coercion)
- ΟΥΔΕΙΣ ΤΩΝ ΑΝΔΡΩΝ ΕΚΕΙΝΩΝ (14:24, "none of those men")
- ΟΥ ΜΙΣΕΙ (14:26, "does not hate" — Semitic idiom for subordination of loyalty)
- ΒΑΣΤΑΖΕΙ ΤΟΝ ΣΤΑΥΡΟΝ ΕΑΥΤΟΥ (14:27, "bears his own cross")
- ΨΗΦΙΖΕΙ ΤΗΝ ΔΑΠΑΝΗΝ (14:28, "count the cost")
- ΑΠΟΤΑΣΣΕΤΑΙ ΠΑΣΙΝ ΤΟΙΣ ΕΑΥΤΟΥ ΥΠΑΡΧΟΥΣΙΝ (14:33, "renounce all his own possessions")
- ΜΩΡΑΝΘΗ (14:34, "becomes tasteless/foolish")
- ΟΥΤΕ ΕΙΣ ΓΗΝ ΟΥΤΕ ΕΙΣ ΚΟΠΡΙΑΝ (14:35, "neither for the earth nor for the dunghill")

### Translation Principles Applied

1. **14:1 — "entered" vs. "came":** EMET renders "when he came into," LOGOS "when he entered into," PNEUMA "Jesus went to eat at." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "entered" is the natural formal rendering of the infinitive construction; PNEUMA restructures the clause entirely.

2. **14:1 — "one of the rulers" vs. "a certain one of the rulers":** EMET renders "a certain [one] of the rulers," LOGOS "one of the rulers," PNEUMA "a prominent Pharisee." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "one of the rulers" is sufficient; "a certain one" (EMET) over-translates *tinos*; PNEUMA collapses the phrase.

3. **14:2 — "who had dropsy" vs. "who was dropsical":** EMET renders "who was dropsical," LOGOS "who had dropsy," PNEUMA "whose body was swollen with fluid." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "who had dropsy" is clearer English than EMET's adjectival form; PNEUMA paraphrases the medical term.

4. **14:3 — "answered and spoke" vs. "answering, spoke":** EMET renders "answering, Jesus spoke," LOGOS "Jesus answered and spoke," PNEUMA "Jesus turned to... and asked." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "answered and spoke" renders *apokritheis eipen* in the standard Hebraistic formula; PNEUMA paraphrases.

5. **14:4 — "let him go" vs. "released him":** EMET renders "released [him]," LOGOS "let him go," PNEUMA "sent him on his way." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "let him go" naturally renders *apelysen*; PNEUMA adds interpretive coloring.

6. **14:5 — "which of you, if a son" vs. "if your son":** EMET renders "Which of you, [if] a son or an ox shall fall," LOGOS "Which of you shall have a son or an ox fall," PNEUMA "If your son or your ox falls." SOPHIA follows EMET's structure with LOGOS's smoother phrasing — the rhetorical "Which of you" preserves the Greek interrogative *tinos*. All three correctly preserve the Sinaiticus reading ΥΙΟΣ ("son") against the variant ΟΝΟΣ ("donkey").

7. **14:5 — "pull him out" vs. "pull him up":** EMET renders "pull him out," LOGOS "pull him up." SOPHIA follows EMET — "pull out" better captures the action of extracting from a well (*phrear*); "pull up" (LOGOS) is also acceptable.

8. **14:6 — "answer him" vs. "answer-back to these things":** EMET renders "answer-back to these [things]," LOGOS "answer him again concerning these things," PNEUMA "had no answer to give him." SOPHIA follows LOGOS in substance, simplified — "answer him concerning these things" is clear without EMET's hyphenation or PNEUMA's paraphrase.

9. **14:7 — "observing" vs. "noticing":** EMET renders "noticing," LOGOS "observing," PNEUMA "when he noticed." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "observing" is more formal and precise for *epechōn*.

10. **14:7 — "first seats" vs. "first-reclining-places":** EMET renders "first-reclining-places," LOGOS "first seats," PNEUMA "seats of honor." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "first seats" renders *prōtoklisias* concisely; EMET's compound hyphenation is unwieldy; PNEUMA interprets rather than translates.

11. **14:9 — "give this one the place" vs. "give place to this one":** EMET renders "Give this [one] the place," LOGOS "Give place to this one," PNEUMA "Give this person your seat." SOPHIA follows EMET — "Give this one the place" is more natural English word order; LOGOS inverts without cause; PNEUMA adds "your seat."

12. **14:10 — "glory" vs. "honor":** EMET and LOGOS render "glory" (*doxa*), PNEUMA renders "honored." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "glory" preserves the theological weight of *doxa* that "honor" (PNEUMA) flattens.

13. **14:10 — "who recline with you" vs. "the other guests":** EMET renders "those reclining-together with you," LOGOS "all who recline with you," PNEUMA "all the other guests." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "who recline with you" preserves *synanakaeimenōn* without EMET's hyphenation or PNEUMA's modernization.

14. **14:11 — "For" vs. "Because":** EMET renders "Because," LOGOS "For," PNEUMA "For." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "For" is the natural causal connector rendering *hoti* in this proverbial context.

15. **14:12 — "a repayment be made to you" vs. "repayment be made to you":** EMET renders "a repayment be made to you," LOGOS "repayment be made to you." Both are faithful; SOPHIA follows EMET — the article "a" renders the sense of *antapodoma* as a single compensatory act.

16. **14:13 — "maimed" vs. "crippled":** EMET and LOGOS render "maimed" (*anapeirous*), PNEUMA renders "crippled." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "maimed" is the more precise equivalent of *anapeiros* (literally "without limbs/dismembered"); "crippled" (PNEUMA) is broader.

17. **14:15 — "when one of those who reclined at table" vs. "a certain one of those reclining-together":** EMET renders "a certain one of those reclining-together," LOGOS "when one of those who reclined at table with him," PNEUMA "when one of the guests at the table." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "who reclined at table with him" is the full formal rendering; EMET's "reclining-together" lacks clarity; PNEUMA simplifies.

18. **14:16 — "But he said" vs. "And he said":** EMET renders "And he said," LOGOS "But he said," PNEUMA "Jesus replied." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "But" (*de*) captures the responsive, corrective force of the parable that follows the guest's platitude.

19. **14:17 — "for all things are now ready" vs. "because all things are now ready":** EMET renders "because [all things] are now ready," LOGOS "for all things are now ready," PNEUMA "because everything is now prepared." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "for" is the natural rendering of *hoti* in reported speech; "all things" (not "everything") maintains formal register.

20. **14:18 — "with one accord" vs. "from one accord":** EMET renders "from one [accord]," LOGOS "with one accord," PNEUMA "one after another." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "with one accord" is the established English idiom for *apo mias*; PNEUMA's "one after another" shifts the meaning from unanimity to sequence.

21. **14:18 — "I have need" vs. "I have necessity":** EMET renders "I have necessity," LOGOS "I have need," PNEUMA "I need." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "I have need" is natural formal English; "necessity" (EMET) is overly stiff.

22. **14:19 — "prove them" vs. "try them out":** EMET and LOGOS render "prove" (*dokimasai*), PNEUMA "try them out." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "prove" preserves the formal register and renders *dokimazō* precisely.

23. **14:21 — "reported" vs. "reported all this":** EMET renders "reported these [things]," LOGOS "reported these things," PNEUMA "reported all this." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "reported these things" is the formal equivalent without PNEUMA's expansion.

24. **14:22 — "Lord" vs. "Sir":** EMET and LOGOS render "Lord," PNEUMA "Sir." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "Lord" preserves the theological resonance of *kyrie* in a parable with allegorical dimensions, consistent with SOPHIA's established practice (cf. 13:8).

25. **14:24 — "who were invited" vs. "who have been invited":** EMET renders "who have been invited," LOGOS "who were invited," PNEUMA "who were originally invited." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "who were invited" renders the perfect participle *keklēmenōn* naturally; PNEUMA adds "originally" beyond the text.

26. **14:25 — "going with him" vs. "going-together-with him":** EMET renders "going-together-with him," LOGOS "going with him," PNEUMA "traveling with Jesus." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "going with him" is clear without EMET's hyphenation or PNEUMA's substitution of the pronoun.

27. **14:26 — "hate":** All three translations preserve *misei* as "hate." SOPHIA retains "hate" — the Semitic idiom must be rendered directly to preserve Jesus' deliberately provocative formulation, with the theological explanation left to commentary.

28. **14:26 — "is not able to be my disciple" vs. "cannot be my disciple":** EMET renders "is not able to be my disciple," LOGOS "is not able to be my disciple," PNEUMA "cannot be my disciple." SOPHIA follows EMET and LOGOS (2-to-1) — "is not able to be" preserves the full force of *ou dynatai einai* and the threefold repetition across vv. 26, 27, 33.

29. **14:28 — "sit down" vs. "sitting-down":** EMET renders "sitting-down," LOGOS "sit down," PNEUMA "sit down." SOPHIA follows LOGOS and PNEUMA (2-to-1) — "sit down" without hyphenation.

30. **14:28 — "whether he has enough to complete it" vs. "if he has enough for its completion":** EMET renders "if he has [enough] for [its] completion," LOGOS "whether he has enough to complete it," PNEUMA "to see whether you have enough to finish it." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "whether he has enough to complete it" is clear formal English; "completion" (EMET) is unnecessarily nominal.

31. **14:29 — "all who see it" vs. "all those beholding":** EMET renders "all those beholding," LOGOS "all who see it," PNEUMA "everyone who sees it." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "all who see it" is natural and concise.

32. **14:31 — "going to encounter" vs. "going to engage":** EMET renders "going to engage another king in war," LOGOS "going to encounter another king in war," PNEUMA "about to go to war against another king." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "encounter" renders *symbalein* precisely (literally "to throw together, to meet in conflict").

33. **14:32 — "while the other is yet far off" vs. "while he is still far-off":** EMET renders "while he is still far-off," LOGOS "while the other is yet far off," PNEUMA "while the other king is still far away." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "while the other is yet far off" clarifies the subject; PNEUMA adds "king" beyond the text.

34. **14:32 — "asks the terms of peace" vs. "asks for the things toward peace":** EMET renders "asks [for] the [things] toward peace," LOGOS "asks the terms of peace," PNEUMA "ask for terms of peace." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "the terms of peace" renders *ta pros eirēnēn* idiomatically.

35. **14:35 — "dunghill" vs. "dung-heap" vs. "manure pile":** EMET renders "dung-heap," LOGOS "dunghill," PNEUMA "manure pile." SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "dunghill" is the established formal rendering of *kopria*; "dung-heap" (EMET) is needlessly hyphenated; "manure pile" (PNEUMA) is colloquial.

36. **14:35 — "He who has ears to hear" vs. "The one having ears to hear":** EMET renders "The [one] having ears to hear, let him hear," LOGOS "He who has ears to hear, let him hear," PNEUMA "Let anyone who has ears to hear, listen!" SOPHIA follows LOGOS — "He who has ears to hear, let him hear" is the classic formal rendering; PNEUMA converts to an exclamation not present in the Greek.

---

*Compiled by SOPHIA in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA, from Codex Sinaiticus (4th century CE).*
